steps.label: 5.1 Double Integration over Rectangular Regions
steps.subtitle: Definition
steps.bodyHtml:
The double integral of the function $f(x,y)$ over the rectangular region R in the xy-plane is defined
            $$\iint_{R} f(x,y) \, dV \, dA=\lim_{m,n \to \infty}\sum_{i=1}^m\sum_{j=1}^n f(x_i^*,y_j^*)\delta A $$
            But what do all of these mean?

steps.label: Rectangular Division
steps.bodyHtml:
Observe that our domain is a rectangle in the xy-plane. We have divided this rectangle into several smaller sub-rectangles. For each of these sub-rectangles, we select a representative point, which we’ll denote by $(x_j, y_j)$. At that point, we evaluate our function $f(x, y)$. The value $f(x_j, y_j)$ gives us the height of a rectangular “box” built above that small region.
By multiplying this height $f(x_j, y_j)$ by the area of the base sub-rectangle, we obtain the volume of that small box.
If we repeat this process for every sub-rectangle and then add up all those small volumes, we get an approximation for the total volume under the surface $z = f(x, y)$ and above the rectangular region in the plane.
As we make the subdivisions finer and finer, this approximation becomes more accurate and in the limit, it becomes the double integral of $f(x, y)$ over the region.

steps.label: Double Integral
steps.subtitle: Visualization in 3D
steps.bodyHtml:
In this GeoGebra visualization, you can see how each of the small boxes is formed above the sub-rectangles of our domain. This picture helps us visualize how the double integral is constructed, by summing up the volumes of all these tiny boxes.
You can think of the double integral as representing the total volume under the surface $z = f(x, y)$. Notice, however, that this interpretation works directly only when the surface lies above the xy-plane, since in that case the function values are positive and the volume makes sense as a positive quantity.

steps.label: Double Integral
steps.subtitle: for General Regions
steps.bodyHtml:
In this GeoGebra visualization, we are looking at a more general region, which we have divided into rectangular strips. (We are not showing the smaller subdivisions this time.)
Notice that, to cover the entire region, these strips extend from the left to the right across the x-values of the region. The height of each strip goes from the bottom curve up to the top curve.
As we imagine taking finer and finer strips, their width becomes smaller and smaller — approaching zero. However, since the width never actually reaches zero, we represent it symbolically as dx.

steps.label: Visualization of a Double Integral over a general region
steps.bodyHtml: Visualization of a Double Integral over a general region

steps.label: Theorem 5.1 Properties of Double Integrals
steps.bodyHtml:
Assume that the functions $f(x,y)$ and $g(x,y)$ are integrable over the rectangular region R; S and T are subregions; and assume that m and M are real numbers.
i. The sum $f(x,y)+g(x,y)$ is integrable and
$$\iint_{R}[f(x,y)+g(x,y)]dA=\iint_{R}f(x,y)dA+\iint_{R}g(x,y)dA$$ ii. If c is a constant, then $cf(x,y)$ is integrable and $$\iint_{R}cf(x,y)dA=c\iint_{R}f(x,y)dA$$ iii. If $R=S\cup T$ and $S\cap T=\emptyset$ except an overlap on the boundaries, then $$\iint_{R}f(x,y)dA=\iint_{S}f(x,y)dA+\iint_{T}f(x,y)dA$$ iv.If $f(x,y)\geq g(x,y)$ for $(x,y)$ in R, then $$\iint_{R}f(x,y)dA \geq \iint_{R}g(x,y)dA$$ v. If $m\leq f(x,y) \leq M $, then $$m\times A(R) \leq \iint_{R}f(x,y)dA\leq M\times A(R)$$ vi. In the case where $f(x,y)$ can be factored as a product of a function $g(x)$ of x only and a function $h(y)$ of y only, then over the region $R=\{(x,y)|a\leq x \leq b, c\leq y \leq d\}$, the double integral can be written $$\iint_{R}f(x,y)dA=\left(\int_{a}^{b}g(x)dx\right)\left(\int_c^d h(y)dy\right)$$

steps.label: Definition
steps.subtitle: Iterated Integrals
steps.bodyHtml:
Assume a,b,c and d are real numbers. We define an iterated integral for a function $f(x,y)$ over the rectangular region $R=[a,b]\times [c,d]$ as
a. $$\int_a^b \int_c^d f(x,y)dydx=\int_a^b \left[\int_c^d f(x,y)dy\right]dx$$ b. $$\int_c^d \int_a^b f(x,y)dydx=\int_c^d \left[\int_a^b f(x,y)dy\right]dx$$

steps.label: Theorem 5.2
steps.subtitle: Fubini's Theorem
steps.bodyHtml:
Suppose that $f(x,y)$ is a function of two variables that is continuous over a rectangular region $R=\{(x,y)\in\mathbb{R}^2|a\leq x\leq b, c\leq y \leq d\}$. Then the double integral of f over the region equals any of the iterated integrals.
More generally, Fubini's theorem is true if f is bounded on R and f is discontinuous only on a finite number of continuous curves. In other words, f has to be integrable over R.

steps.label: Theorem 5.4
steps.subtitle: Fubini's Theorem Strong Form
steps.bodyHtml:
For a function $f(x,y)$ that is continuous on a region D of Type 1 (a region that lies between two vertical lines), we have
$$\iint_{D}f(x,y)dA=\iint_{D}f(x,y)dydx=\int_a^b\left[\int_{g_1(x)}^{g_2(x)}f(x,y)dy\right]dx$$ Similarly, for a function $f(x,y)$ that is continuous on a region D of Type II (a region that lies between two horizontal lines) we have $$\iint_{D}f(x,y)dA=\iint_{D}f(x,y)dxdy=\int_c^d\left[\int_{h_1(y)}^{h_2(y)}f(x,y)dx\right]dy$$

steps.label: Example 5.15
steps.subtitle: Changing the order of integration
steps.bodyHtml:
Changing the order of itegration is one of the typical exercises to assess understanting of double
            integrals.
